segments.title: Could Britain afford the G3 and N3 programmes?
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke argues that Britain could build capital ships after the First World War and surrendered useful Washington treaty leverage by cancelling too early.
segments.body: The answer distinguishes inability to spend from a political choice not to spend. Shipbuilding could provide employment during demobilization, while the cruisers then completing were largely wartime programmes rather than a competing wave of new construction. Dr. Clarke treats the cancelled G3 and N3 hulls as negotiating assets and explores converting G3s into large carriers instead of abandoning them before the Washington talks.
segments.question: Was cancellation safer because postwar Britain could not afford both new battleships and cruisers?
segments.answerShort: Britain was financially constrained but still capable of building; cancelling before negotiations discarded leverage and possible carrier-conversion hulls.

evidence.note: The comment and response cover affordability, employment, older-ship disposals, treaty leverage, and G3 carrier conversions.

segments.title: Nelson displacement and the N3 speed estimate
segments.summary: A design discussion asks why Nelson and Rodney remained below the treaty limit and revisits how fast an N3 hull might have been.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke suggests that another 1,200 tons needed a worthwhile purpose and that a declared figure below the ceiling also suited Britain's treaty presentation. He accepts a speed estimate around 26 knots and says his own hull-form calculation came out somewhat higher. His main reservation is not the ships' overall quality but the all-forward main-battery arrangement.
segments.question: Why were Nelson and Rodney about 33,800 tons rather than the full 35,000, and how fast might N3 have been?
segments.answerShort: The unused margin offered limited benefit and diplomatic value, while N3's hull form plausibly supported roughly 26 to 28 knots.

evidence.note: The exchange compares treaty displacement, possible uses for extra tonnage, hull form, horsepower, and gun placement.

segments.title: Japan's carrier options after the Amagi loss
segments.summary: The Amagi earthquake loss opens an alternate treaty path toward a purpose-built Japanese fleet carrier and larger interwar carrier norms.
segments.body: The answer considers whether Japan could receive a 27,000- or 33,000-ton replacement after operating Akagi and Kaga, potentially producing an unusually powerful purpose-built carrier. It then follows the consequences into later treaty negotiations and British carrier requirements. A 33,000-ton British design might combine the survivability of an armored carrier, Ark Royal's strike capacity, and Unicorn's distant-support role more economically than two smaller ships, though Dr. Clarke stresses that this is speculative history.
segments.question: What might Japan have built after the earthquake destroyed Amagi in a treaty regime with more large carrier conversions?
segments.answerShort: Japan might have negotiated a purpose-built carrier of 27,000 to 33,000 tons, affecting both prewar capability and later treaty limits.

evidence.note: The response explores Amagi's loss, replacement tonnage, later treaties, and British large-carrier roles.

segments.title: G3 blind arcs and concentrated turret firepower
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke weighs the G3's stern blind arc against the unusually large share of firepower concentrated in each triple turret.
segments.body: He accepts the stated firing restrictions but emphasizes that the ship could have no main guns covering some aft angles. With triple turrets, losing one arc or mount represents far more firepower than on a four-twin layout. Despite that criticism, he still regards the G3 as a strong paper design and a particularly attractive basis for carrier conversion.
segments.question: Was the G3's X turret blind over only angles where other British ships also had limited fire?
segments.answerShort: The angles were real, but a triple turret concentrated so much firepower that the uncovered stern sector remained a significant design penalty.

evidence.note: The exchange describes blind arcs, twin-versus-triple turret firepower, and the overall G3 assessment.

segments.title: Why an unrestricted gun race might revive mixed batteries
segments.summary: Barrel wear and slow fire lead to a speculative battleship combining very heavy guns with faster 13.5-inch weapons.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke agrees that practical gun size is constrained by muzzle velocity and severe barrel wear. He imagines an unrestricted race pushing beyond 18 inches, then rediscovering a pre-dreadnought-like mixed battery because the largest weapons fire too slowly. In that scheme, very heavy shells damage armor first and quicker 13.5-inch fire exploits the weakened area, but the resulting ship would be enormous and operationally awkward.
segments.question: Would barrel wear and muzzle-velocity limits impose a practical ceiling on battleship gun size?
segments.answerShort: Yes; attempts to exceed 18 inches could force a mixed battery so faster medium-caliber heavy guns could exploit damage made by the largest shells.

evidence.note: The answer links railway-gun barrel wear to a speculative 18-inch and 13.5-inch mixed armament.

segments.title: Names for additional Lexington-class carrier conversions
segments.summary: A brief naming aside considers Constellation and Constitution for additional American battlecruiser-to-carrier conversions.
segments.body: The cancelled Lexington-class battlecruiser names provide plausible identities for hypothetical third and fourth conversions. Dr. Clarke especially likes USS Constitution and notes that the converted group might informally have become known as the Constitution class of aircraft carriers.
segments.question: Which names might additional Lexington-class carrier conversions have received?
segments.answerShort: Constellation and Constitution are attractive candidates, with Constitution potentially lending its name to the carrier class.

evidence.note: The comment lists cancelled battlecruiser names and the response favors Constitution for a carrier.

segments.title: All-forward main guns versus the G3 and N3 Q turret
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke rejects a Nelson-style all-forward arrangement as worse than the G3 and N3 Q-turret scheme.
segments.body: The answer is concise but clear: he understands why Nelson and Rodney adopted their arrangement under treaty constraints, yet would not prefer it for a G3- or N3-derived design. The watch point captures his hierarchy between two unconventional main-battery layouts.
segments.question: Would a Nelson-like all-forward G3 or N3 be better than the Q-turret design?
segments.answerShort: No. Dr. Clarke regards the all-forward solution as worse, despite understanding why it was used on Nelson and Rodney.

evidence.note: The question directly compares an all-forward layout with the Q-turret design.

segments.title: Books for studying interwar treaty warships
segments.summary: John Jordan's treaty-warship books, Brassey's annuals, and naval-institute transactions are recommended for deeper study.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke points viewers toward John Jordan's volumes on warships after Washington and London. He also recommends contemporary Brassey's material from 1919 and the 1920s and the transactions of naval institutes. Together these offer both later synthesis and period professional discussion.
segments.question: What books or sources are useful for studying post-First World War designs and the naval treaties?
segments.answerShort: Start with John Jordan's Washington and London treaty-warship books, then consult period Brassey's volumes and naval-institute transactions.

evidence.note: The response names John Jordan's books, Brassey's, and naval-institute transactions.

segments.title: Could the X Kiwi design fit a 35,000-ton limit?
segments.summary: A proposed 1920s battleship could fit the treaty ceiling by redistributing armor and rearranging its secondary battery.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke believes the design is feasible with 1920s technology, but only after deliberate compromises. He proposes thinning and spreading armor, using water as part of the protection scheme, moving secondary turrets, and shifting the superstructure and funnel. The resulting broadside would still combine nine 16-inch guns with eight 6-inch guns, though he would like more machinery power if weight allowed.
segments.question: Could the proposed X Kiwi Force design be built with 1920s technology below 35,000 tons?
segments.answerShort: Probably, by carefully redistributing armor and rearranging the secondary armament and superstructure, while accepting tight weight tradeoffs.

evidence.note: The answer details armor, secondary-turret, superstructure, and machinery tradeoffs for the treaty limit.

segments.title: G3 design lessons and carrier-conversion potential
segments.summary: The closing comparison argues that G3 ideas could improve Nelson and Rodney and imagines the hull as a capacious aircraft carrier.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke praises the technology and design thinking embodied in G3 while reiterating his objections to unconventional turret placement. He sketches an alternative raised secondary-turret arrangement and retains triple 16-inch guns for a battleship version. Looking at the hull as a carrier conversion, he emphasizes the enormous flight deck and hangar volume it could provide while retaining useful secondary and anti-aircraft weapons.

evidence.note: The conclusion compares G3 with Nelson and Rodney and visualizes the stripped hull as an aircraft carrier.
